codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of POST /api/memos multipart upload endpoint. The code is well-structured with clear separation between API layer (memos.py), service layer (upload.py), and schemas (upload.py). 79 test cases provide thorough coverage of the 17 validation assertions. A few non-blocking observations noted below.

issues.description: Module-level `DATA_DIR = get_settings().data_dir` evaluates at import time. Tests work around this by monkey-patching `upload_mod.DATA_DIR` in the fixture, but this pattern is fragile — any new module importing from upload.py before the fixture runs would get the wrong path. The existing system module uses the same pattern, so this is consistent, but a dependency-injection approach would be more robust.

No file size limit enforced at the application level. While nginx/reverse proxy or FastAPI middleware could handle this, there is no explicit guard against extremely large uploads (e.g., multiple GB). This is a low-risk concern for a local/self-hosted app but worth noting for robustness.

The filename is stored as-is in `source_filename` in the database and used in the title derivation. While no filesystem operations use the raw filename (storage uses UUID-based paths), the `source_filename` column is unbounded — no length check. Extremely long filenames could cause issues in display contexts. Low risk for a self-hosted app.

The `title` parameter is a single Form field applied to ALL files in a batch. When uploading multiple files with a custom title, all memos get the same title. This matches the feature description (single optional title param) but may not be ideal UX for batch uploads. Acceptable for now; per-file titles could be a future enhancement.

File write `source_path.write_bytes(file_content)` is synchronous and blocks the event loop. For the current scope (local app, typical audio files), this is fine. If very large files become common, this should use `aiofiles` or run in a thread executor. Not blocking.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker used module-level `DATA_DIR = get_settings().data_dir` pattern in the upload service, matching the existing pattern in `app/main.py` and `app/api/system.py`. The test fixture monkey-patches `DATA_DIR` on each module. This pattern is now used in 3 modules. AGENTS.md mentions 'Use plain sqlite3 with connection management via FastAPI Depends' but the actual pattern is module-level config + monkey-patching in tests, not DI via Depends.

The upload service opens and closes a DB connection per file in batch uploads (each `create_memo_and_job` call does `conn = get_connection(db_path)` + `conn.close()`). For batch uploads this means N separate connections for N files. This works but differs from a connection-per-request pattern. Not documented as a convention either way.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: backend/app/services/upload.py:65-87 — get_connection/conn.close inside the for loop

sharedStateObservations.observation: The `_now_iso()` helper in upload.py uses `strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ')` which produces microsecond precision. The DB schema uses `DEFAULT (strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ', 'now'))` for the same format. This is consistent, but worth noting that `%f` in Python's strftime gives microseconds (6 digits) while SQLite's `%f` may behave differently. In this case both are Python-controlled so it's fine, but worth being aware of.

Worker followed the backend-worker skill procedure correctly: read context, wrote tests first (TDD), implemented to make tests pass, ran quality checks (ruff format, ruff check, ty check), ran full test suite, did manual curl verification. skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true and no deviations were noted. The skill procedure matches reality well.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff verification commands show all steps completed in order

summary: The upload endpoint implementation is complete and well-tested. The code correctly handles single/batch upload, format validation (7 supported formats, 422 for unsupported), optional parameters (title, language, hotwords, enable_diarization), default title derivation from filename, file storage at /app/data/memos/{id}/source.original, and special characters in filenames. Security is solid — files are stored using UUID-based paths (not raw filenames), preventing path traversal. The 79 test cases cover all 17 assigned validation assertions. Non-blocking observations: module-level DATA_DIR pattern is fragile but consistent with existing code; no explicit file size limit at app level; synchronous file I/O in async handler is acceptable for local-app scope. Overall: PASS.
